references.title: Predicting University Students’ Academic Success and Major Using Random Forests

extracted.Courses: All courses at University of Toronto
extracted.Features: [StudentID, Number of Credits attempted- Divided by Department and average grade across Department ]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each dataset has 142 features since there are 71 Departments at University of Toronto.

Target variable is computed according to the rule: Registered for 5 credits, did not succeed in at least 18 and did not take a course in 3 semesters were defined as dropouts. 

Authors also perform a feature importance analysis via Permutation Descrease Importance.

Permutation is preferred since it does not show to be a reliable indicator of variable importance in different types of predictors.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.7884 - Random Forest #3, Precision: 0.5295 - Random Forest #3, Recall: 0.7500 - Random Forest #3}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use the term class-specific Accuracy, which by the text seems to be precision.

Authors fiund RF to be the best model even though they attempt 3 forms of RF.
extracted.Limitations:
Use of Grades to predict success. 

Does not consider option of a student being in multiple majors or specializations. 

Inneficient addressing of Nans.

Holdout method is split in 90%-5%-5%, which is unorthodox.

annotations.text: The authors take 10 years worth of data from a Chinese university and create 2 Random Forest models. The first model is meant to predict end-of-program success just from using data from the first 2 semesters. The second RF is used for interpretation purposes as it is used to understand in which courses the successful students first registered.
